content: Concept One-liner --- --- Stock An ownership claim on a company's residual cash flows — you get what's left after everyone else has been paid. Bond A loan that you can trade — the issuer borrows money, pays you regular interest, and returns principal at maturity. FX rate The price of one currency in terms of another — EUR/USD = 1.08 means one euro costs $1.08. Commodity A standardized physical good (oil, gold, wheat) traded on exchanges, mostly via futures rather than physical delivery. Credit The risk that a borrower won't pay you back — traded via corporate bonds (cash) and credit default swaps (synthetic). Duration How much your bond's price changes for a 1% move in rates — duration 7 means ~7% loss per 1% rate rise. Convexity The curvature of the price-yield relationship — tells you how inaccurate your duration estimate will be for large moves. Credit spread The extra yield a corporate bond pays above the risk-free Treasury rate — it's the price tag of default risk. Carry trade Borrow in a low-rate currency, invest in a high-rate currency, pocket the difference — works until a violent reversal. CDS An insurance contract — buyer pays a regular premium and gets paid if the reference company defaults. Crowding Too many funds own the same stocks — works until everyone tries to exit through the same door at the same time. Contango Front month cheaper than later months — storage costs make future delivery more expensive. Negative roll yield. Backwardation Front month more expensive than later months — tight supply now creates a scarcity premium. Positive roll yield. Yield curve Plots interest rates by maturity — tells you what the market believes about growth, inflation, and policy. ---

Master equation: Price = Earnings × Multiple Valuation Method What it does Limitation --- --- --- Relative (P/E, EV/EBITDA) Compare to peers/history "Cheap vs expensive" is relative, not absolute DCF Discount future cash flows at WACC 60-80% of value comes from terminal value — tiny assumption changes → huge output swings Sum-of-parts Value each segment separately Ignores synergies ---

Metric Formula Meaning Trap --- --- --- --- P/E Price / EPS Years of earnings you're paying for Useless for negative earnings Forward P/E Price / Next yr EPS Paying for future earnings Only as good as the estimate EV/EBITDA (Mkt cap + Debt - Cash) / EBITDA Price of operating business regardless of leverage Ignores capex FCF Yield FCF / Mkt cap Cash return on investment. Higher = cheaper Can be manipulated Dividend Yield Div / Price Current income High yield can signal distress ("yield trap") Short Interest Shares short / Float Bearish positioning. >15% elevated, >25% squeeze risk High SI might be correct Beta Cov(stock,mkt) / Var(mkt) Market sensitivity. 1.3 = 30% more volatile than market Backward-looking, changes in stress ---

Factor What it is --- --- Market (beta) Systematic risk — the tide that lifts or sinks all boats. Not diversifiable. Sector Same-industry correlation. 30 stocks isn't diversified if 20 are tech. Idiosyncratic Company-specific: earnings miss, fraud, CEO departure. Diversifiable across 30+ names. Value Cheap vs expensive. Outperforms long-term, can underperform for years. Momentum Recent winners keep winning — until violent reversals. Size Small caps outperform historically but with much higher volatility. Quality High-profitability companies are defensive in downturns. ---

Type Key feature Unique risk --- --- --- Treasury Risk-free benchmark Rate risk only Corporate IG Spread above Treasuries Rate + credit Corporate HY High yield, significant default risk Trades like equity in a crisis MBS Prepayment risk → negative convexity Lose on both sides: rates up (stuck) and rates down (prepaid early) TIPS Principal adjusts with CPI Real yield instrument Floating rate Coupon resets with SOFR + spread Very low duration Convertible Can convert to equity Hybrid: bond floor + equity upside. Has embedded option. ---

Measure Use case --- --- Coupon rate Cash income only. Not return. Current yield Better — uses market price. Ignores gain/loss at maturity. YTM Standard measure. Assumes reinvestment at same rate (unrealistic). YTW Use for callable bonds — issuer calls at worst time for you. Spread to benchmark Isolates credit risk. What credit traders actually watch. ---

Type What it measures --- --- Macaulay Weighted-average time to receive cash flows (years) Modified Price sensitivity: ΔP/P ≈ -D × Δy Effective For bonds with embedded options (MBS, callables) Key rate (KRD) Sensitivity to specific curve points (2yr, 5yr, 10yr, 30yr) What increases duration: Longer maturity, lower coupon, lower yield, zero coupon (duration = maturity). DV01 = Duration × Market Value × 0.0001 → dollar loss per 1bp rate rise. ---

Shape What it signals --- --- Normal (upward) Healthy economy, term premium Flat Slowing economy, uncertain outlook Inverted Market expects rate cuts → recession signal (every US recession since 1970 had one) Bear flattening Fed hiking aggressively → pre-recession Bull steepening Short rates falling faster → early easing ---

> Extract zero-coupon (spot) rates from observed market prices, one maturity at a time, each using previously solved rates. Maturity Instrument Method --- --- --- O/N – 6M T-bills, SOFR, repo Direct observation (zero-coupon → yield IS the spot rate) 6M – 2Y SOFR futures (SR3) Futures price → forward rate → compound to spot rate 2Y – 30Y Interest rate swaps Set PV(fixed leg) = 1, solve for unknown spot rate using known shorter rates The bootstrap logic: For an n-year swap at par rate $c$: - Discount each coupon by the spot rates you already know ($z 1, z 2, ..., z {n-1}$) - Solve for $z n$ — the only unknown - Repeat for each successively longer maturity What you get from spot rates: - Discount factors: $DF(T) = 1/(1+z T)^T$ — price ANY fixed cash flow - Forward rates: Market-implied rate between two future dates - Par rates: Coupon at which a bond prices at 100 (= the swap curve) Post-2008: Multi-curve bootstrapping — SOFR for discounting, separate curve for projection. Two curves, not one. RM relevance: Every derivative is priced off this curve. Different interpolation methods → different forward rates → different derivative prices and hedge ratios. ---

Rating Spread (bps) Default risk --- --- --- AAA/AA 20-60 Negligible — spread is mostly liquidity premium A 60-120 Very low BBB 120-200 Cliff edge — downgrade to BB triggers forced selling BB 200-400 Junk. First to blow out in stress. B 400-700 Significant default probability CCC+ 700+ Distressed. Trades on recovery value. IG vs HY: IG = rate bet. HY = equity bet in a bond wrapper. Don't confuse them. ---

Pair Personality --- --- EUR/USD Most liquid. US vs Eurozone rates/growth. Stable trends. USD/JPY Risk appetite + carry trade. Violent snap-backs on unwinds. BOJ intervention above 155. GBP/USD UK economy. More volatile. Flash crash risk. USD/CNH China managed float. Fix signals policy intent. EM (TRY, ZAR, BRL) EM risk barometers. Can move 5-10% in days. Liquidity vanishes in stress. DXY Dollar index (EUR 57.6%, JPY 13.6%, GBP 11.9%, CAD 9.1%). Standard "USD strength" reference. FX drivers (in order): Rate differentials → Growth differentials → Central bank policy → Risk appetite → Trade/capital flows → Intervention. ---

Commodity What drives it Key fact --- --- --- Crude Oil OPEC+, China demand, geopolitics (Hormuz), inventory Most important commodity — feeds into inflation, rates, FX, equities Natural Gas Weather, storage, LNG Extremely volatile. Amaranth blew up here ($6B loss). Gold Real rates (inverse), USD (inverse), safe haven Real rate hedge, NOT an inflation hedge per se Copper China industrial demand (50%+), EV transition "Doctor Copper" — leading indicator of global growth Wheat/Corn Weather, trade policy, fertilizer Supply shocks can be enormous (Ukraine 2022) Unit conversions: 1 barrel = 42 gal. 1 WTI contract = 1,000 barrels. 1 gold contract = 100 troy oz. 1 corn contract = 5,000 bushels. ---

- Buy protection: Pay spread quarterly. If default → receive (par - recovery). - Sell protection: Collect spread. Bear default risk. = Being long the credit. - Spread widening = more default risk = bearish. - CDS-bond basis = CDS spread minus cash bond spread. Large gap = liquidity stress or structural issue. Example: 5yr CDS at 200bps on $10M notional → buyer pays $200K/yr. If default with 40% recovery → buyer receives $6M. ---

section: RM RED FLAGS BY ASSET CLASS
content: Asset Class Red flag --- --- Equities Gross >200%, top 5 >25%, hidden factor concentration, any position >10% ADV Fixed Income DV01 exceeding budget, >80% in one maturity bucket, large negative convexity unaware FX Large unhedged position not matching PM view, concentrated EM carry Commodities Large long in deep contango (bleeding roll), approaching expiry with no roll plan Credit Sudden CDS widening in portfolio names, >30% BBB on negative watch, rising default rate >3%

Concept One-liner --- --- Physical Real barrels/tonnes changing hands for near-term delivery — producers, consumers, trading houses Cash/Forward OTC contracts for physical delivery priced vs a benchmark Futures Exchange-traded, standardized, margined — what financial players trade Contango Front month cheaper than back months — storage costs. Negative roll yield (you bleed on every roll) Backwardation Front month more expensive — tight supply now. Positive roll yield (you earn on every roll) Crack spread Product price minus crude price — the refiner's gross margin Spark spread Power price minus (gas price × heat rate) — the gas generator's margin Crush spread (Soy meal + soy oil revenue) minus soybean cost — the processor's margin Basis Price at a specific location vs the benchmark — reflects local supply/demand and logistics Heat rate Efficiency of converting gas to electricity (MMBtu/MWh). Lower = more efficient Merit order Generators dispatched cheapest to most expensive. The marginal unit sets the price for all Roll yield Gain or loss from rolling an expiring futures contract to the next month — can dominate returns ---

Benchmark Exchange Settlement What it represents --- --- --- --- WTI NYMEX (CL) Physical @ Cushing, OK US domestic. Light sweet. Can go negative (did in April 2020). Brent ICE (B) Cash-settled Global benchmark. ~75% of world crude priced off Brent. Dated Brent OTC (Platts) Physical North Sea cargoes The PHYSICAL benchmark. Futures converge to this. Dubai/Oman DME Physical (Oman crude) Asian sour crude benchmark. Prices Middle East → Asia flows. Brent-WTI spread: Normally Brent +$2-5. Reflects Atlantic Basin balance. Blew out to $25 in 2011 (Cushing glut). Crude quality: API gravity (higher = lighter = more valuable). Sulfur (sweet <0.5%, sour >0.5%). Light sweet commands a premium. ---

Product Exchange/Ticker Size Season What it is --- --- --- --- --- RBOB Gasoline NYMEX (RB) 42,000 gal Summer (May-Sep) Motor fuel ULSD (Diesel) NYMEX (HO) 42,000 gal Winter (heating) Diesel + heating oil Jet Fuel OTC (vs Platts/ULSD) No standard Year-round Aviation fuel, priced as crack to ULSD Fuel Oil (HSFO/VLSFO) ICE, SGX Various Year-round Shipping fuel. IMO 2020 split into VLSFO (0.5% S) and HSFO (3.5% S) Crack spreads: - Simple: Product − Crude - 3-2-1: (2 × RBOB + 1 × ULSD − 3 × Crude) / 3 = US Gulf refiner margin - Wide cracks → refiners maximize throughput → pulls crude demand - 2022 diesel cracks hit $70+/bbl (normal $15-25) — Russia sanctions ---

Region Benchmark Exchange Unit Price Range --- --- --- --- --- US Henry Hub (HH) NYMEX (NG) $/MMBtu ~$2-4 (cheap, shale) Europe TTF ICE Endex €/MWh Volatile ($30-340 in 2022) UK NBP ICE p/therm Trades vs TTF spread Asia JKM (Platts) OTC $/MMBtu Competes with TTF for LNG cargoes Conversion: 1 MMBtu ≈ 0.293 MWh. $3/MMBtu ≈ €9.5/MWh (rough). Key concepts: - Associated gas: Gas from oil drilling. More oil drilling → more gas → gas prices FALL even as energy demand rises - Storage report: EIA every Thursday 10:30 AM ET. Market trades deviation from consensus. - LNG arbitrage: Cargoes flow to whoever pays more — JKM > TTF → cargoes go to Asia; TTF > JKM → cargoes to Europe - Oil-indexed LNG: Asian long-term contracts priced as ~14.5% of JCC crude. $80 oil → $11.60 gas. - TTF became global LNG benchmark after 2022 energy crisis

Hub Where What it reflects Basis to HH --- --- --- --- Henry Hub Erath, Louisiana THE benchmark. 13 pipelines converge. — Waha West Texas (Permian) Oversupplied associated gas, pipeline-constrained Deep discount (sometimes negative) Dominion South Appalachia (Marcellus) Shale gas production, pipeline bottlenecks Discount Algonquin Citygate New England Northeast heating demand. Winter spikes. Massive winter premium ($10-30+ in polar vortex) SoCal Citygate Southern California West Coast power generation Moderate premium Chicago Citygate Midwest Heating + industrial. Well-connected. Small premium or parity AECO Alberta, Canada Canadian gas, depends on US export pipes Discount Transco Zone 6 NYC delivery Urban Northeast. Extreme winter premiums. Premium Katy Hub SE Texas Gulf Coast industrial + LNG export demand Near parity Why basis matters: In the 2014 polar vortex, HH = $4.50 while Algonquin = $78 — a 17x difference. Hedging New England exposure with HH futures covers almost nothing when pipelines are maxed. LNG export terminals: Sabine Pass (~4.2 Bcf/d), Freeport (~2 Bcf/d), Cameron, Calcasieu Pass, Corpus Christi. Feed gas data (gas flowing into terminals) is watched daily — drops signal operational issues. ---

ISO/RTO Region Key Feature --- --- --- PJM Mid-Atlantic + Midwest Largest US market. Capacity market. Coal/gas mix. ERCOT Texas (isolated) NO capacity market → extreme spikes. $9,000/MWh in Feb 2021 freeze. CAISO California Solar-heavy. Duck curve: oversupply midday, spike at sunset. NYISO New York Congested transmission. NYC ≠ upstate prices. ISO-NE New England Winter gas/power spikes (pipeline constraints). MISO Midwest Wind-heavy. Low prices when windy. SPP Central Plains Very wind-heavy. Negative prices when oversupplied. Key formulas: - Implied heat rate = Power price / Gas price → profitability of burning gas - Spark spread = Power − (Gas × Heat rate) → generator margin. Negative = uneconomic to run. - Dark spread = Power − (Coal × Emission cost) → coal generator margin. Negative prices: Power can go negative when renewables overproduce and generators pay to stay connected. ---

Metal LME Code Lot Size Key Driver Approx Price --- --- --- --- --- Copper CA 25 t China (50%+), EVs, green transition $8,000-10,000/t Aluminium AH 25 t Energy costs (smelting), China cuts $2,200-2,800/t Zinc ZS 25 t Galvanizing steel, mine supply $2,400-3,000/t Nickel NI 6 t Stainless steel, EV batteries, Indonesia $15,000-25,000/t Lead PB 25 t Car batteries, declining demand $2,000-2,300/t Tin SN 5 t Electronics soldering $25,000-35,000/t LME is unique: 3-month benchmark, daily prompt dates, physical delivery via LME warehouses, open-outcry ring. COMEX Copper: 25,000 lbs. Cents/lb. Convert: LME $/tonne ÷ 2,204.6 × 100 = cents/lb. Doctor Copper: Leading growth indicator — copper goes into everything (wiring, plumbing, EVs, grids). 2022 Nickel squeeze: Tsingshan short → Russia war spiked nickel → $25K to $100K in 24 hours → LME cancelled trades → credibility destroyed. ---

Metal Exchange Lot Size Key Driver --- --- --- --- Gold COMEX 100 troy oz Real rates (INVERSE), USD (inverse), central bank buying Silver COMEX 5,000 troy oz Gold ×0.8 correlation + industrial (solar, electronics) Platinum NYMEX 50 troy oz Diesel autocatalysts, hydrogen fuel cells, South Africa (70%) Palladium NYMEX 100 troy oz Gasoline autocatalysts, Russia (40%), South Africa Gold is NOT an inflation hedge — it's a REAL RATE hedge. Watch 10yr TIPS yield: falls → gold rises. Rises → gold falls. LBMA Gold Price: Set twice daily (10:30 AM, 3:00 PM London). Used by central banks, ETFs, physical contracts. Gold-Silver ratio: >80 = stressed. <65 = risk-on. ---

Product Lot Size Unit Key Driver --- --- --- --- Corn 5,000 bu cents/bu Ethanol (40% of US corn), Iowa/Illinois weather, China Soybeans 5,000 bu cents/bu China crush (70% of global soy imports), Brazil competition Wheat (SRW) 5,000 bu cents/bu Black Sea exports (Russia/Ukraine 30%), weather Soybean Oil 60,000 lbs cents/lb Biodiesel, palm oil competition Soybean Meal 100 short tons $/ton Animal feed Cotton 50,000 lbs cents/lb China/SE Asia textiles, US weather Live Cattle 40,000 lbs cents/lb Cattle cycle, feed costs, drought

Product Lot Size Key Feature --- --- --- Milling Wheat 50 t European benchmark (Euronext Paris) Rapeseed 50 t European oilseed, biodiesel (Euronext Paris) White Sugar 50 t Refined sugar (ICE London, $/t) Cocoa 10 t West Africa 60%+ supply. 2024: prices tripled (disease + weather) Robusta Coffee 10 t Vietnam 1. Instant coffee / blending (ICE London) Arabica Coffee 37,500 lbs Brazil 1. A single frost → +40% (ICE US)

Product Exchange Key Feature --- --- --- Palm Oil Bursa Malaysia 25 t, MYR/t. Malaysia + Indonesia = 85%+ of global supply. Most consumed veg oil. Rubber TOCOM / SGX Thailand + Indonesia dominate. 70% for tires. Competes with synthetic (oil-derived). Iron Ore SGX / DCE 100 t. China = 70%+ of seaborne imports. Really a China property/infra bet. WASDE Report: USDA monthly supply/demand estimates. The most market-moving ag report. Even 1-2M tonne revisions can move prices 3-5%. Key growing season: April-June planting → July-August critical weather → Sep-Nov harvest. Midwest drought in July = corn/soy spike 30%+. ---

Spread What it tells you --- --- Calendar (time) Curve shape, storage economics, supply outlook Crack (products − crude) Refining margin Spark (power − gas × HR) Gas generation margin Crush (meal + oil − beans) Soybean processing margin Frac (NGL − gas) NGL extraction economics Brent-WTI Atlantic Basin crude balance Brent-Dubai (EFS) Light-sweet vs heavy-sour, Atlantic vs Pacific JKM-TTF Asian vs European LNG, cargo diversion direction Location basis (gas) Local pipeline/weather vs benchmark Gold-Silver ratio Risk appetite (>80 stressed, <65 risk-on) ---

Red Flag Why it matters --- --- Position >5% of open interest You ARE the market — liquidation moves prices against you Large position near expiry, no roll plan Operational disaster — especially physically-settled (WTI) Large long in deep contango Bleeding roll cost every month regardless of spot "Diversified" commodities that are all growth bets Long crude + NatGas + copper = one macro bet, not three Unmonitored basis risk Hedging jet fuel with WTI ≠ hedged Margin increases during vol Forces liquidation at worst time Geopolitical binary (Hormuz, Black Sea) Overnight gaps with no hedge Fighting seasonality without thesis Long NatGas in April needs a strong reason

Concept One-liner --- --- Derivative A contract whose value is derived from an underlying asset — lets you take exposure without owning it Future A binding agreement to buy/sell at a set price on a set date — both sides OBLIGATED Option The RIGHT (not obligation) to buy/sell at a set price by a set date — premium is the price of that right Call Right to BUY at the strike. Want underlying UP. Put Right to SELL at the strike. Want underlying DOWN. Swap OTC agreement to exchange cash flows over time — typically fixed for floating IRS Exchange fixed-rate for floating-rate payments. Most traded derivative in the world. TRS Get total economic exposure (price + dividends) WITHOUT owning the asset — synthetic leverage (Archegos) CDS Insurance against default — buyer pays premium, seller pays if default occurs Variance swap Pure vol bet — pays realized variance minus strike. Short variance = unlimited downside (Volmageddon) Implied vol The vol the market is pricing into an option — backed out from the market price via Black-Scholes Put-call parity C − P = Forward. A call minus a put = synthetic forward position. Arbitrage relationship. ---

Type Exercise Pricing Model Where Used Cost vs European --- --- --- --- --- European Only at expiry Black-Scholes (closed-form) Index options (SPX), FX, most OTC Baseline American Any time before expiry Binomial tree (check early exercise at each node) US single-stock options (AAPL, MSFT) ≥ European (early exercise right has value) Bermudan Specific dates (e.g., quarterly) Lattice / Monte Carlo Swaptions, callable bonds Between European & American Asian At expiry, payoff on AVERAGE price Monte Carlo (path-dependent) Commodity hedging, FX hedging < European (averaging reduces vol) Barrier Knock-in/out at a price level Analytical or Monte Carlo Structured products, FX, commodities Cheaper (conditional activation) Digital At expiry, fixed payout if ITM Modified BS Structured products Depends on structure Why different models? BS works when you know WHEN exercise happens. Add optionality about WHEN (American) or path-dependency (Asian) → need numerical methods (trees, simulation). ---

Factor ↑ Calls Puts Why --- --- --- --- Higher implied vol More expensive More expensive More uncertainty → option has higher expected value More time to expiry More expensive More expensive More time for something to happen Higher interest rates More expensive Cheaper Forward price increases Higher dividends Cheaper More expensive Forward price decreases ATM strike Most time value Most time value Maximum uncertainty about ITM/OTM Deep OTM strike Cheap Cheap Low probability of payoff ---

Price = Intrinsic Value + Time Value Component Call Put --- --- --- Intrinsic max(0, S − K) max(0, K − S) Time value Premium − Intrinsic Premium − Intrinsic Moneyness: Deep ITM (δ ≈ ±0.90–1.00) → ITM → ATM (δ ≈ ±0.50, max gamma/time value) → OTM → Deep OTM (δ ≈ ±0.01–0.10) ---

Inputs: Spot (S), Strike (K), Time (T), Rate (r), Dividend yield (q), Volatility (σ) Output: Option price + Greeks Where it breaks: - Fat tails (crashes more frequent than bell curve) - Vol isn't constant (vol surface exists) - No continuous trading (gaps on earnings, weekends) - No early exercise (need binomial for American) ---

Strategy View Max Gain Max Loss Greeks --- --- --- --- --- Long Call Bullish Unlimited Premium +Δ, +Γ, −Θ, +ν Short Call Neutral/Bearish Premium Unlimited −Δ, −Γ, +Θ, −ν Long Put Bearish Strike − premium Premium −Δ, +Γ, −Θ, +ν Short Put Neutral/Bullish Premium Strike − premium +Δ, −Γ, +Θ, −ν Pattern: Long options = +Γ, +ν, −Θ (pay rent, get protection). Short options = −Γ, −ν, +Θ (collect rent, bear tail risk). ALWAYS.

Strategy Construction View Key Insight --- --- --- --- Bull call spread Buy low-K call + sell high-K call Moderately bullish Caps upside, reduces cost. Defined risk. Bear put spread Buy high-K put + sell low-K put Moderately bearish Caps profit, cheaper than outright. Straddle Buy ATM call + ATM put Big move, direction unknown Most expensive vol bet. Double theta. Strangle Buy OTM call + OTM put Big move, cheaper than straddle Wider breakevens, needs bigger move. Collar Long stock + buy put + sell call Protect downside, cap upside Can be zero-cost. Most common hedge. Covered call Long stock + sell OTM call Income on existing position Synthetically = short put (put-call parity). Butterfly Buy 1 low + sell 2 mid + buy 1 high Pin at middle strike Cheap defined-risk low-vol bet. Iron condor Bear call spread + bull put spread Range-bound Defined-risk premium selling. Risk reversal Sell OTM put + buy OTM call Strong directional Zero/near-zero cost. Measures skew. Calendar spread Sell near-dated + buy far-dated (same K) Theta decay play Near decays faster than far.

Situation Strategy --- --- Bullish, limited budget Bull call spread Protect downside, hate paying premium Zero-cost collar or put spread Earnings: big move, don't know direction Long straddle or strangle Vol too high, stock stays in range Iron condor or short strangle Max directional conviction Risk reversal Income on stock I own Covered call Pin at specific price Butterfly ---

Underlying Exchange Ticker Size Settlement --- --- --- --- --- S&P 500 E-mini CME ES $50 × Index Cash Nasdaq E-mini CME NQ $20 × Index Cash 10Y Treasury CBOT ZN $100K face Physical WTI Crude NYMEX CL 1,000 bbl Physical (Cushing) Brent Crude ICE B 1,000 bbl Cash Gold COMEX GC 100 troy oz Physical EUR/USD CME 6E €125,000 Cash Futures pricing: F = S × e^(r−q)T. Futures = Spot + carry cost − income from holding. ---

Swap Fixed side Floating side Wins when --- --- --- --- IRS (pay fixed) Pay 4% Receive SOFR Rates rise IRS (receive fixed) Receive 4% Pay SOFR Rates fall TRS Pay SOFR + spread Receive total return on asset Asset appreciates CDS (buy protection) Pay spread Receive par − recovery if default Credit deteriorates Commodity swap Pay fixed price Receive floating commodity price Commodity falls ---

Exotic What it is Key risk --- --- --- Barrier Knock-in/out at a level Pin risk near barrier, delta jumps Asian Payoff on AVERAGE price Cheaper, lower vol Variance swap Pure vol bet Short = unlimited downside Swaption Option on an IRS Vol-of-rates Convertible Bond + embedded equity call Complex Greeks, arb strategy ---

Risk What happens Example --- --- --- Leverage $500K premium controls $20M notional. Losses scale to notional. Barings (Leeson), futures leverage Non-linearity (gamma) Losses ACCELERATE: delta × move + gamma × move²/2 Any short gamma blowup Liquidity illusion Tight spreads in calm → no quotes in stress OTC derivatives in March 2020 Model dependence Model wrong → "hedged" position isn't hedged Feb 2018 vol models, LME nickel 2022 Margin cascade Vol up → margin up → forced selling → more vol → spiral Amaranth, MF Global Counterparty risk Counterparty defaults → you lose unrealized gains AIG 2008 ($182B bailout)

Concept One-liner --- --- Trade lifecycle Order → route → execute → clear (CCP novation) → settle (delivery vs payment) Market order Guaranteed execution, NOT guaranteed price. Can cross multiple levels in a thin book. Limit order Guaranteed price, NOT execution. You may never get filled. CCP Steps between buyer and seller → eliminates bilateral risk but creates concentration risk Novation CCP replaces original contract with two: A→CCP and CCP→B Settlement US equities T+1 (since May 2024). European equities T+2. FX spot T+2. ISDA Constitution of OTC derivatives — governs netting, close-out, events of default CSA Collateral framework — thresholds, eligible collateral, haircuts, rehypothecation Initial margin Deposit to OPEN a position. Performance bond. Variation margin Daily mark-to-market settlement. Losing side pays. Maintenance margin Minimum level. Below → margin call (post to IM, not maintenance). Pro-cyclical margin Exchanges RAISE margin in vol spikes → demand cash when you can least afford it Margin cascade Margin call → forced selling → price drops → more margin calls → spiral Repo Collateralized short-term loan. Sell securities today, agree to buy back tomorrow. Haircut Discount on collateral: post $100M bonds, receive $98M cash (2% haircut). Gross leverage (Longs + Shorts) / NAV → total exposure Net leverage (Longs − Shorts) / NAV → directional exposure Market liquidity Can you sell at fair value in size? Bid-ask, depth, ADV, impact. Funding liquidity Can you access cash? Credit lines, repo, margin availability. Liquidity spiral Market illiquidity → funding pressure → forced selling → more illiquidity → doom loop Short squeeze Forced buying feedback loop — shorts cover → price rises → more shorts forced to cover Days to cover Short interest / ADV. Above 10 = crowded short, squeeze risk. Gamma flip Price where dealer gamma goes from positive (dampening) to negative (amplifying moves) ---

Type Guarantees Risk Use case --- --- --- --- Market Execution ✓ Bad price in thin books Small/urgent, liquid names Limit Price ✓ No fill if moves away Have a target, can wait Stop Exit (once triggered) Gap through stop level Automatic risk management Stop-limit Price floor (once triggered) May not execute at all Want stop but refuse to sell below floor MOC Closing price Must submit before cutoff Benchmark to close, rebalancing Iceberg Stealth Can be detected by algos Large orders, minimize info leakage ---

Algo How Best for --- --- --- VWAP Volume-profile weighted Benchmark to VWAP TWAP Even time intervals Even execution Implementation Shortfall Front-loaded Minimize slippage from decision price Liquidity-seeking Finds pockets of liquidity Minimize impact at all costs POV (% of Volume) Fixed % of real-time volume Adapt to live conditions Market impact ∝ √(order size / ADV). Double size → ~40% more impact. ---

CCP Clears --- --- DTCC/NSCC US equities, corporates CME Clearing Futures, cleared IRS LCH (SwapClear) ~90% of global cleared IRS ICE Clear Energy futures, CDS OCC US listed equity options Eurex Clearing Euro Stoxx, Euro rates CCP waterfall: Defaulter's IM → Defaulter's default fund → CCP capital → Other members' default fund → Assessment. ---

Market Cycle --- --- US equities T+1 US Treasuries T+1 European equities T+2 FX spot T+2 (CLS Bank) Futures Daily MTM OTC derivatives Per ISDA/CSA Herstatt risk: Time zone mismatch in FX settlement → CLS Bank created to settle simultaneously. ---

Term What it means --- --- Netting Multiple trades → single net amount if default. Reduces exposure 80-90%. Close-out All trades terminated and netted on credit event. No cherry-picking. Greek One-liner Analogy --- --- --- Delta (Δ) Dollars gained/lost per $1 move in underlying. Your effective stock-equivalent position. Volume knob — controls how loud the stock plays through to your P&L. ITM=cranked to 10. ATM=halfway. OTM=barely a whisper. Gamma turns the knob. Gamma (Γ) How fast delta changes. Acceleration of P&L. THE most important Greek for risk. Airbag — long Γ deploys on impact (cushions + profits from crashes). Short Γ = airbag ripped out (damage accelerates). Theta (Θ) Daily rent for holding optionality. Long options pay it, short options earn it. Parking meter — ticks slowly far from expiry, eats quarters near expiry. Are you feeding it or do you own it? Vega (ν) P&L per 1-point change in implied vol. Your exposure to fear itself. Weather forecast, not the weather — you profit from the forecast getting scarier, not the storm itself. Rho (ρ) P&L per 1% change in interest rates. Minor for short-dated, material for LEAPS/FX. River current — short kayak trip = barely notice; multi-day expedition = carries you miles off course. Vanna How delta shifts when vol moves. Your hedges get stronger or weaker when fear arrives. Shape-shifter — directional exposure morphs when vol regime changes. Volga How vega changes when vol moves. Long volga = tail protection (vol convexity). Vol convexity — does a vol spike make things better or worse? Progressively. Charm Delta drifts overnight from time passing. Critical near expiry and over weekends. Silent thief — steals your hedge overnight while you sleep. Speed How gamma changes with spot. Important near barriers. Gamma of gamma — is your gamma itself stable or about to shift? Color How gamma evolves with time. Gamma spikes into expiry. Gamma's clock — measures how fast gamma concentrates approaching expiry. Dollar delta Δ × contracts × multiplier × spot. The REAL number — not option delta. — Dollar gamma P&L for 1% move from gamma = ½ × Γ$ × (%move)². Squared → accelerating. — Gamma/theta Efficiency metric: how much gamma per dollar of theta. Low = overpaying for optionality. — ---

Gamma: "Gamma tells you WHERE YOU'LL BE after a move. Delta tells you where you are NOW. For risk, gamma matters more." Gamma-theta: "No free lunch. Long gamma = pay daily rent for crash protection. If IV was 25% and realized is 10%, you lose on vega crush despite being 'right' about the event."

Concept One-liner --- --- Realized vol Measures how much the asset ACTUALLY moved — the backward-looking truth. Implied vol The market's consensus forecast of future volatility — backed out of option prices. It's the weather forecast, not the weather. Vol risk premium Implied vol is almost always higher than subsequent realized vol. The difference is the insurance premium — what makes selling vol profitable on average, and catastrophic in the tail. Skew Measures how much more expensive downside protection is relative to ATM options — it's the market's fear of crashes priced in real-time. Term structure Tells you whether the market expects current vol conditions to persist or mean-revert. Vol regime Vol doesn't just have a level — it has a REGIME. Regime shifts are the most dangerous events in finance because everything correlated changes simultaneously. VIX The 30-day expected volatility of SPX, derived from option prices. You CAN'T trade it directly — only through derivatives on VIX. Variance swap Pure vol bets — linear in VARIANCE (= vol²), which makes them convex in vol. Long variance is a crash bet on steroids. Vol vs correlation Vol tells you how much individual assets move. Correlation tells you whether they move TOGETHER — and in a crisis, they all do. ---

Concept Definition Key fact --- --- --- Realized vol StdDev of actual returns × √252 Backward-looking. Lookback window matters (5d vs 20d vs 252d). Implied vol Vol backed out of option prices (solve BS for σ) Forward-looking. Contains expected vol + VRP. VRP (Vol Risk Premium) IV − subsequent RV +2-4 pts SPX average. Positive ~85% of months. Makes selling vol profitable on average, catastrophic in tails. IV ≠ expected vol. IV = expected RV + insurance premium. ---

> One-liner: VaR is the maximum loss at a given confidence level over a given horizon under NORMAL conditions — and it says absolutely nothing about what happens when conditions stop being normal. 99% 1-day VaR = $5M → "On 99/100 days, loss ≤ $5M. On day 100, it's WORSE — and VaR says nothing about how much worse."

> One-liner: A VaR model that's never tested is just an expensive guess. Backtesting asks: "How many times did actual losses exceed VaR?" and if the answer is too many (or too few), the model is broken. 99% VaR, 250 days → expected exceptions: 2.5/year Exceptions Basel zone Action --- --- --- 0-4 🟢 Green Model OK 5-9 🟡 Yellow Under review. VaR limitations: "Speedometer, not crash detector. Five flaws: tail-blind, assumes normality, backward-looking, not subadditive, gameable. Supplement with ES, stress tests, and judgment." ES vs VaR: "Both have same VaR = $5M. Portfolio A's ES = $34M (short OTM puts — concave). Portfolio B's ES = $6.5M (diversified equity — linear). Only ES reveals Portfolio A is 5× more dangerous." Stress testing: "Three layers: historical (did we survive 2008?), hypothetical (what if Taiwan?), sensitivity grids (where does P&L break?). Fourth: reverse stress test — start from the loss, find the scenario. If it's plausible, hedge now." Sharpe: "Standard metric but three fatal flaws: symmetric vol penalty, short-vol gaming, small-sample noise. Always check Sortino — if Sortino << Sharpe, there's negative skew hiding." Drawdown: "−20% needs +25% to recover. −50% needs +100%. That's why hard limits exist — past −20%, recovery becomes a multi-year project. Past −50%, the fund is done."
Quick reference for correlation, diversification, factors, concentration, basis, tail, liquidity, construction, and the RM dashboard ---

Concept One-liner --- --- Correlation A chameleon — it looks stable in calm markets and shape-shifts violently the moment you need it to stay put. Diversification The only free lunch in finance — until the restaurant closes during a crisis and you discover everyone was eating at the same table. Factor models Look through the 500 individual positions and tell you the 8 real risks driving the fund — because 500 positions with the same factor exposure is not diversification, it's a leveraged bet on that factor. Concentration A portfolio with 100 names can be more concentrated than a portfolio with 10 — if those 100 names all load on the same factor, you don't own 100 bets, you own one. Basis risk The risk that your insurance policy pays out on a DIFFERENT house than the one that burned down. Fat tails Normal distributions say a 5-sigma daily move happens once every 14,000 years. Markets produce them every few years. Any risk framework built on normality is a framework for losing money in the tail. Liquidity risk A position isn't risky because it can lose value — every position can lose value. It's risky because you might not be able to EXIT when you desperately need to. RM vs PM A PM constructs portfolios for return. The RM constructs CONSTRAINTS for survival. The intersection — portfolios that maximize return subject to risk constraints — is where the real edge lives. Dashboard Your cockpit — the 20 numbers that tell you whether the fund is safe or whether you need to act before the next market open. ---

Quick reference for Greek-based and factor-based P&L decomposition, patterns, P&L explain, and fraud detection ---

Concept One-liner --- --- P&L attribution Splits yesterday's dollar outcome into the building blocks that caused it — so you know whether the PM was RIGHT about their thesis, LUCKY from market moves, or EXPOSED to a risk they don't understand. Greek vs factor Greek-based attribution tells you WHICH INSTRUMENTS drove P&L. Factor-based attribution tells you WHICH BETS drove P&L — market direction, sector rotation, style tilt, or genuine stock selection. P&L explain Compares what your model PREDICTED the P&L should be against what ACTUALLY happened. The gap is your model error — and persistent gaps mean your risk system is lying to you. Signature reading Certain P&L signatures are diagnostic — like a doctor reading an ECG, the RM reads daily P&L to diagnose the risk the PM is actually taking. Daily brief The PM wants to know three things: Am I up or down? Why? What changed in my risk? Deliver all three in 30 seconds or less. Fraud detection Every major rogue trading scandal has one thing in common — the P&L didn't match the risk. If P&L attribution had been taken seriously, the fraud would have been caught earlier. ---

$$\text{Total P\&L} = \Delta \text{P\&L} + \Gamma \text{P\&L} + \Theta \text{P\&L} + \mathcal{V} \text{P\&L} + \rho \text{P\&L} + \text{Cross} + \text{Unexplained}$$ Component Formula What it measures --- --- --- Delta Σ δ i × ΔS i Directional bet — was the PM right? Gamma ½ Σ γ i × (ΔS i)² Convexity payoff — always positive for long γ Theta Σ θ i × Δt Time decay — rent for holding options Vega Σ ν i × ΔIV i Vol change — is PM secretly a vol trader? Rho Σ ρ i × Δr Rate impact — small for short-dated, big for FI Cross Vanna, charm, volga, speed Normally <5% of total. 20-30% in crises. Unexplained Actual − Attributed Red flag if >5%. Investigate immediately if >15%. Theta-gamma breakeven: $\Delta S {BE} = \sqrt{2 \theta /\gamma}$ — move needed for gamma to offset theta. ---

Ratio What it reveals Red flag --- --- --- Vega % of total Vol trader or directional? >50% → PM is trading vol, not stocks Theta % of total How much is carry income? >70% consistently → short gamma collector Gamma / Theta (cumulative) Fair rent for gamma? <0.5 → overpaying for protection Cumulative theta / Tail loss Pennies-vs-steamroller check <0.1 → earning $2M to risk $20M Delta % of total Pure directional bettor? >90% → just riding beta Factor / Total Alpha or factor exposure? >80% → delivering factor, not skill Unexplained / Total Model working? >15% → escalate Cross-effects / Total Higher-order risk material? >20% → crisis regime, need full repricing > Quick diagnosis: Theta = 75% of P&L → short gamma collector (Sharpe looks great, tail will destroy). Vega = 87% of P&L → vol trader who might not know it. ---

"Explain a PM's daily P&L?" → "Two dimensions: Greek-based (delta = direction, gamma = convexity, theta = time, vega = vol) and factor-based (market, sector, style, macro → residual alpha). If PM thinks they're trading direction but 60% is vega → they're a vol trader. If 90% is momentum factor → they're selling factor at alpha prices. Unexplained >5% → model is missing something or worse." "Unexplained P&L keeps growing?" → "Escalating red flag. Quantify (2% = noise, 15% = broken model). Check direction (always negative = DANGEROUS). Diagnose: stale prices? Wrong Greeks? Missing risk factor? If it persists after data/calibration checks → model whose VaR and stress tests should not be trusted." "Short gamma — how to identify?" → "P&L signature: consistent positive theta, near-zero gamma P&L on calm days, then massive loss on big-move day. Sharpe looks great pre-blowup. Track cumulative theta vs worst-case tail. If $2M theta vs $20M tail loss → 10:1 against. Ensure drawdown limits can absorb the tail." "Alpha vs factor?" → "Multi-factor regression. R² shows how much is explained. Alpha intercept needs t-stat >2.0 + 3-4 years of data. Plot cumulative alpha vs factor over time — flat alpha + rising total = pure factor delivery."

Concept One-liner --- --- No free hedge Every form of protection extracts payment in some currency — premium, upside, basis risk, complexity, or illiquidity. The RM's job is to make the currency explicit. Diagnose first Before picking an instrument, diagnose the disease. A put doesn't help if the risk is basis. A futures hedge doesn't help if the risk is convexity. Match the hedge to the SPECIFIC risk. Static vs dynamic Static hedging is "set and forget." Dynamic hedging is "adjust as conditions change." Both have costs — dynamic costs execution/slippage; static costs missed optimization opportunities. Greek impact Adding a hedge doesn't just reduce risk — it changes your ENTIRE Greek profile. A put adds gamma, theta, vega, and cross-Greeks that interact with the existing book. Roll cost Rolling a hedge is not neutral — you're closing one position at a loss (theta decayed) and opening a new one at market price (potentially higher IV). The roll is where hedge cost compounds. False confidence A hedge that doesn't perform in stress is worse than no hedge — because it created FALSE CONFIDENCE that the position was protected. ---

Concept One-liner --- --- Sizing The PM decides WHAT to trade. The RM helps decide HOW MUCH. Sizing is the largest single driver of portfolio P&L variance. Liquidity ceiling You can't size a position larger than you can EXIT. Liquidity is not a soft constraint — it's a hard wall. Conviction Isn't "I feel strongly." It's a structured assessment of variant perception, catalyst, downside, and edge durability — each of which can break independently. Risk budget A CONCESSION of trust from the firm. Breach it and you lose trust. Manage it well and you earn more. RM monitoring role The PM builds the portfolio. The RM monitors whether the portfolio the PM ACTUALLY has matches the portfolio they MEANT to have. Position lifecycle Every position has a birth, a life, and a death. The RM tracks all three stages with a different lens at each. Scaling The PM's primary expression of changing conviction. The RM ensures scaling decisions are CONSCIOUS, not reactive. Risk contribution A 5% position in a high-beta, high-correlation stock might contribute 20% of portfolio VaR. The RM's job is to make RISK CONTRIBUTION visible, not just dollar weight. Morning dashboard Every morning, the RM should be able to answer 10 questions about the book in under 60 seconds by looking at their dashboard. ---

Concept One-liner --- --- PM vs trader bias Traders face a different bias landscape than PMs because they make HUNDREDS of decisions per day. PM biases compound over weeks. Trader biases compound within hours. RM immunity You're not immune. Recognizing your OWN biases is what separates an effective RM from a compliance box-ticker. Detection Don't wait for the P&L to tell you there's a bias problem. Build a detection system that flags behavioral patterns BEFORE they destroy returns. Fixing biases You can't fix biases by telling people to be less biased. That doesn't work. You fix them by building STRUCTURAL countermeasures into the trading process. Confronting PMs Telling a PM "you're biased" is like telling someone "you're ugly." It's accurate but counterproductive. The skill is in making the pattern visible without triggering defensiveness. ---

section: Quant Tools Applied to Discretionary Risk
content: Tool Application --- --- Factor decomposition "60% of variance is momentum — you have factor risk, not alpha" Regime detection "High-vol regime → adjust position sizes to reflect higher per-position VaR" Correlation heatmap "PC1 explains 70% of variance — one bet, not eight" Scenario engine "500 scenarios: worst 10 all involve China hard landing" Alt data "Short interest hit 25% — headline risk incoming"

- Why discretionary RM at a systematic firm? "Judgment matters most day-to-day. I use the quant toolkit (factor decomp, stress tests, correlation) to support a PM where each position has a story, catalyst, and thesis I can audit. Quantitative rigor + human judgment intersection." - Cross-desk correlation? "Weekly regression of disc returns vs systematic factors. R² > 0.3 → doubled up, not diversified. Present data to PM + CRO with solutions: factor hedge, idiosyncratic shift, or gross reduction." - Biggest challenge? "Credibility both ways. CRO wants quant language; PM wants behavioral intelligence. Infrastructure built for 3,000 positions needs qualitative overlays for 40. Supplement VaR with stress + scenario + thesis audit."

Concept One-liner --- --- Encapsulation Bundle data and methods together and HIDE internal state — callers use the public interface, they can't touch the guts. Inheritance Child classes get parent behaviour for free and can override it — Equity IS-A Instrument , so it automatically has ticker , quantity , price and only needs to implement what's different. Polymorphism One function works on many types — portfolio risk summary() calls .market value() on every item in the list and doesn't care whether it's an Equity , Bond , or Option . Abstraction Define WHAT a class must do (interface) without saying HOW — Instrument declares market value() as @abstractmethod ; you can't instantiate it until you've implemented the method. Composition Prefer "has-a" over "is-a" — Portfolio HAS a list of Instrument objects; it doesn't inherit from Instrument . Looser coupling, easier to change. @property A method that looks like an attribute — limit.utilization computes on the fly, always consistent, no risk of stale stored state. @staticmethod A utility function that lives on the class but needs no instance or class state — Instrument.from bloomberg ticker("AAPL US") . @classmethod An alternative constructor that receives the class as first arg — Position.from csv row(row) lets you build objects from different data sources. repr What you see in the debugger — implement it so positions print as Equity(AAPL, qty=1000, px=185) not <object at 0x...> . eq / hash Required to put objects in sets or use them as dict keys — define equality by ticker so two Equity("AAPL", ...) objects match.

Principle One-liner --- --- Single Responsibility One class = one reason to change — RiskCalculator computes risk; it does NOT format reports. Open/Closed Open for extension, closed for modification — add CDS(Instrument) without touching Equity . Liskov Substitution A child must be usable wherever its parent is — if code takes Instrument , passing an Option must work correctly. Interface Segregation Don't force a class to implement methods it doesn't need — a thin Priceable interface beats one giant FullInstrument interface. Dependency Inversion Depend on abstractions, not concretions — RiskEngine depends on the RiskModel protocol, not HistoricalVaRModel directly.

Pillar Key idea Risk example --- --- --- Encapsulation Private state, public interface RiskLimit. breach history — can't modify externally Inheritance Parent → Child Instrument → Equity , Bond , Option Polymorphism Same interface, different behavior portfolio risk summary() works for any Instrument Abstraction ABC with @abstractmethod Instrument.market value() — every instrument must implement ---

Pattern One-liner definition Risk use case --- --- --- Strategy Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each one, and make them interchangeable at runtime without changing the calling code. Swap VaR method (hist/param/MC) at runtime — same engine.compute() call, different model injected. Observer When one object changes state, all registered dependents are automatically notified — publisher/subscriber without tight coupling. Limit breach → automatically triggers email alert + dashboard update + audit log. Factory Centralise object creation logic so callers don't need to know which concrete class to instantiate. InstrumentFactory.create("equity", row) returns the right class based on instrument type from the DB. Decorator Wrap a function or class to add behaviour (logging, caching, validation) without modifying its source code. @timer measures execution, @validate returns checks for NaNs — both stack on top of compute var() . Pipeline Chain a sequence of processing steps where each step's output is the next step's input. Load → Clean → Calculate → Report — each stage is independent and testable. - Payoff: max(A − K, 0) where A = arithmetic average over option life - Why corporates love them: smooths price spikes (jet fuel hedging over a year, not one day) - Pricing: No closed-form (avg of log-normals ≠ log-normal). Use Turnbull-Wakeman or Monte Carlo. - Turnbull-Wakeman: Moment-matching. Fit log-normal with same mean + variance as true arithmetic average → plug into BS. Fast, accurate for vanilla structures. - Fixed vs floating strike: Fixed = max(A−K, 0). Floating = max(S T−A, 0) — pays if final spot > average (captures momentum).

Greek Definition Key risk --- --- --- Delta ∂V/∂S Directional exposure Gamma ∂²V/∂S² Re-hedging cost, spikes near expiry ATM Vega ∂V/∂σ Vol exposure Theta ∂V/∂t Daily time decay, accelerates √T near expiry Vanna ∂²V/∂S∂σ Delta changes as vol moves — crash risk Volga ∂²V/∂σ² Vega convexity. Deep OTM = high volga. Vol spike → vega shifts. Theta non-linear: Near expiry, ATM theta ∝ 1/√T — accelerates sharply. Worst case: short ATM near expiry in quiet market.

- BS intuition: Delta-hedge portfolio is riskless → earns risk-free rate → BS PDE. Closed-form = N(d1), N(d2). - Itô's lemma: Chain rule for stochastic processes. df = (∂f/∂t + μ∂f/∂X + ½σ²∂²f/∂X² )dt + σ∂f/∂X dW. The bold term = Itô correction = gamma in options . - Risk-neutral pricing: Change of measure so every asset earns r. Drift disappears → no expected return input needed. Price = E^Q payoff × discount . - Delta hedging = dynamic replication: Continuously rebalance (Δ shares + cash) to replicate option payoff. Cost of replication = option premium.

- DV01: ModDur × Price × 0.0001. Example: ModDur=8, $1M face → DV01 = $800/bp. Additive across positions. - Macaulay duration: Weighted avg time to cash flows (years) - Modified duration: Macaulay / (1 + y/m) = % price change per unit yield change - Convexity correction: ΔP ≈ −ModDur×Δy + ½×Convexity×(Δy)². Always positive for plain bonds — good to own. - Negative convexity: Callable bonds + MBS. Issuer/borrower calls when rates fall → you're short a call → price capped on rally. VaR underestimates risk for MBS in falling rate environments. ---

Concept One-liner --- --- Conditional probability The answer changes based on WHAT you condition on — "at least one heads" ≠ "first flip heads." Always draw the sample space. Bayes' theorem Prior × Likelihood / Evidence. A 99% accurate test with 0.01% prevalence has ~1% PPV. Base rates dominate. Expected value Probability-weighted average outcome. Positive EV ≠ good bet if variance is extreme (St. Petersburg). Geometric vs arithmetic +20% then −20% = −4%, not 0%. Geometric return ≈ arithmetic − σ²/2. Vol destroys compounding. Gambler's ruin With a slightly negative edge, you go broke with near certainty. Even with positive edge, overleveraging kills. Fermi estimation Decompose → estimate each factor → multiply → sanity check. Errors cancel across multiplicative factors. ±3x is a good Fermi estimate. Optimal stopping Observe the first 37% (1/e), then pick the next one that beats all you've seen. Works for hiring, entry timing, and hedge shopping. Ergodicity Positive EV per trial but negative geometric growth → you still go broke. Kelly criterion fixes this. Average speed trap Average speed = total distance / total time (harmonic mean), NOT the arithmetic mean of speeds. You can never average 2× your first-lap speed. ---
